name: Montgomery County, Maryland
tagline: Maryland's largest and wealthiest county — ~1.05M residents in the DC suburbs, a ~$7.9B operating budget. Enterprise IT runs through the Department of Technology & Enterprise Business Solutions (TEBS); buying flows through the County's Office of Procurement with County Council oversight.
overview: Montgomery County is Maryland's most populous and most affluent jurisdiction — roughly 1.05 million residents in the Washington, DC suburbs — governed by an elected County Executive and an 11-member County Council, with day-to-day operations run by a Chief Administrative Officer. Enterprise technology is centralized in the Department of Technology & Enterprise Business Solutions (TEBS), which owns IT governance, enterprise systems, cybersecurity, and the County's broadband/fiber assets; the Office of Enterprise Information Security (OEIS) sits inside TEBS. Like most large local governments, the County is a partly federated buyer: TEBS sets enterprise strategy and runs shared infrastructure, while large departments (Police, Fire & Rescue, Health & Human Services, and the separately governed Montgomery County Public Schools) carry their own substantial technology needs. For a Dell SLG seller, the County is a high-value, well-funded DC-metro local-government opportunity with an unusually mature technology posture — but the entry point is TEBS plus the County's Office of Procurement and Council-approval process, not a single capital line. Note an active leadership transition: County Executive Marc Elrich is term-limited and a new executive will take office in late 2026 following the 2026 election cycle.

budget.intro: The County Council approved a $7.9B FY2027 operating budget on May 21, 2026 (effective July 1, 2026), rejecting the County Executive's proposed property-tax-rate increase and adopting a progressive income-tax structure instead. Public education remains the dominant line — more than $3.7B for Montgomery County Public Schools, about half the total. Central IT is a small share of that: TEBS's own recommended operating budget has run about $73M (~227 FTEs). As with most large counties, technology spend is spread across departments and capital (CIP) projects rather than concentrated in one IT appropriation.

stats.label: FY2027 operating budget
stats.note: Approved by Council May 21, 2026; effective July 1, 2026

stats.label: MCPS share (FY2027)
stats.note: Public schools ~half the County budget; MCPS runs its own IT

notes: Technology spending is partly federated — large departments and the separately governed Montgomery County Public Schools run their own systems, so there is no single county-wide 'IT budget' line to target. Confirm a department's own funding before forecasting.

Infrastructure modernization tends to flow through the Capital Improvements Program (CIP) — e.g., FiberNet and technology-services projects — which is on a separate multi-year approval track from the operating budget. The FY27 Council review flagged reduced Technology Services CIP funding and warned of rising deferred-maintenance and cybersecurity risk.

A new County Executive takes office in late 2026 (Elrich is term-limited; Councilmember Will Jawando won the June 2026 Democratic primary and faces GOP nominee Esther Wells in the Nov. 3 general). Budget priorities for FY2028 and beyond could shift with the transition. Verify the latest adopted figures at time of engagement.

initiatives.name: AI Action Plan & responsible-AI adoption
initiatives.summary: The County published an AI Action Plan and an AI Progress Report (Sept 2025) framing responsible, equitable AI adoption, and partnered with InnovateUS to upskill employees on safe generative-AI use. An active, executive-visible priority and an opening for AI-infrastructure and data-platform conversations.

initiatives.name: Data-center migration to a Tier IV facility (Equinix)
initiatives.summary: TEBS has been migrating the County data center to an Equinix Tier IV facility and modernizing network infrastructure — a direct fit for infrastructure refresh, colocation/hybrid-cloud, and as-a-service plays. Confirm current phase and what remains in flight.

initiatives.name: FiberNet / MoCoNet broadband & digital equity
initiatives.summary: TEBS operates FiberNet (the County's fiber backbone for government and schools) and MoCoNet (free residential broadband at affordable-housing sites), and runs ultraMontgomery/digital-equity programs. FiberNet is a recurring CIP project — an infrastructure and network-modernization track.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity resilience (OEIS)
initiatives.summary: The Office of Enterprise Information Security runs the County's security program; recent modernization work materially reduced attack surface and cut recovery time after incidents. Security is a standing investment area and a clear buying center.

procurement.intro: Selling to Montgomery County runs through its centralized Office of Procurement under the County's Procurement Code (Chapter 11B of the County Code). Solicitations post through the Maryland eMaryland Marketplace / BidNet Maryland Purchasing Group. The County can also buy via intergovernmental cooperative purchasing to extend other jurisdictions' pricing, and larger or noncompetitive actions draw County Council attention. Confirm which department owns the requirement, then map the Office of Procurement path and any usable co-op vehicle.

vehicles.name: Office of Procurement (Chapter 11B)
vehicles.detail: The County's central purchasing function — runs formal and informal solicitations and administers contracts under the County Procurement Code. The primary path for most County buys.

vehicles.name: eMaryland Marketplace / BidNet (MD Purchasing Group)
vehicles.detail: Where County solicitations and award information are posted; the registration and bid-submission channel for vendors.

vehicles.name: Intergovernmental cooperative purchasing
vehicles.detail: The County may purchase off cooperative/intergovernmental contracts to extend pre-negotiated pricing — often faster than a stand-alone County solicitation where a usable co-op vehicle exists. Confirm the specific contract's eligibility for the County.

vehicles.name: County Council oversight
vehicles.detail: The Council enacts the Procurement Code and reviews appropriations and certain contract actions (including noncompetitive/sole-source awards) — factor Council timing into larger deals. Verify current thresholds in Chapter 11B before forecasting.

powerStructure.name: Marc Elrich
powerStructure.title: County Executive (term-limited; leaving late 2026)
powerStructure.note: Heads the executive branch and proposes the budget. Term-limited after voter-approved two-term limits. Councilmember Will Jawando won the June 2026 Democratic primary (rivals conceded) and faces GOP nominee Esther Wells in the Nov. 3 general election; the Democratic nominee is heavily favored in this deep-blue county. Rebuild the executive-sponsor map after the election; verify at engagement.

powerStructure.name: Gail Roper
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer, TEBS
powerStructure.note: CIO leading TEBS since February 2020; owns IT governance, enterprise systems, security strategy, AI innovation, and broadband. The top enterprise-IT relationship for the County.

powerStructure.name: Keith Young
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), Office of Enterprise Information Security
powerStructure.note: Leads OEIS and the County's information-security program inside TEBS — the security buying-center owner. Confirm current title/status before forecasting.

powerStructure.name: County Council (11 members)
powerStructure.title: Legislative branch & budget/contract oversight
powerStructure.note: Adopts the operating and capital budgets, enacts the Procurement Code, and reviews larger/noncompetitive contract actions. The Council's Government Operations (GO) committee typically handles TEBS items.

powerStructure.name: Office of Procurement
powerStructure.title: Central purchasing authority
powerStructure.note: Runs County solicitations and administers contracts under Chapter 11B — the procurement gatekeeper for most County buys, including the co-op and competitive paths.

news.title: Elrich signs 6-month moratorium on new data-center permits
news.impact: June 12, 2026 executive order pauses acceptance/processing of new commercial data-center permits while the Council weighs zoning-text amendments on energy, water, and noise impacts. This targets private/commercial data centers — distinct from the County's own TEBS data-center modernization — but signals a politically charged local stance on data-center growth to factor into any DC-metro siting/infrastructure conversations.

news.title: Will Jawando wins Democratic primary for County Executive
news.impact: Councilmember Will Jawando won the June 2026 Democratic primary (rivals Friedson and Glass conceded) and faces GOP nominee Esther Wells in the Nov. 3 general; the Democrat is heavily favored. A new executive takes office in late 2026 (Elrich term-limited), which can reset budget and technology priorities — re-map the executive relationship after the election.

news.title: Council approves $7.9B FY2027 operating budget
news.impact: May 21, 2026 vote sets the FY2027 envelope (effective July 1, 2026); the Council rejected the Executive's property-tax increase and adopted a progressive income-tax structure, with MCPS funded above $3.7B. IT spend stays spread across departments and the CIP.

news.title: County releases AI Progress Report under its AI Action Plan
news.impact: AI is an active, executive-visible priority — TEBS runs an AI Center of Excellence and resident-facing tools (e.g., the 'Monty 2.0' 311 assistant) — an opening for AI-infrastructure, data-platform, and responsible-AI conversations.

news.title: TEBS recognized for FiberNet/broadband and data-center modernization
news.impact: Confirms an active infrastructure-modernization track (Tier IV data center, network upgrades, digital equity) — an infrastructure and network entry point.

dellActions: Anchor the relationship with TEBS — CIO Gail Roper for enterprise strategy and CISO Keith Young (OEIS) for the security buying center — while mapping department-level needs (Police, Fire, HHS) and the separately governed MCPS.
Lead infrastructure with the data-center modernization story: position ISG, colocation/hybrid-cloud, and refresh against the Equinix Tier IV migration and FiberNet/network upgrades.
Tie AI-infrastructure and data platforms to the County's AI Action Plan and 2025 AI Progress Report — responsible-AI adoption is executive-visible and funded as a priority.

Get the procurement path right early: confirm Office of Procurement (Chapter 11B) routing, the eMaryland Marketplace/BidNet registration, and any usable intergovernmental cooperative-purchasing vehicle; factor Council timing for larger or noncompetitive actions.

Track the County Executive transition (Elrich term-limited; Democrat Will Jawando won the June 2026 primary and is favored in the Nov. 3 general) and rebuild the executive-sponsor map after the election before forecasting large FY2028+ programs; note the June 2026 data-center permit moratorium as a signal of local sensitivity on data-center growth.
